skill.name: gene-database
skill.description: Query NCBI Gene through E-utilities and Datasets APIs for gene symbols, IDs, annotations, and batch lookup workflows.
skill.summary: Search and retrieve NCBI Gene records for research annotation workflows.
skill.author: K-Dense-AI

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found no malicious intent or covert exfiltration. The confirmed risks are expected NCBI network access, user-provided API key handling, and a user-selected output file write.

low_findings.title: Expected Network Access to Official NCBI APIs
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW_RISK. The scripts make outbound HTTPS requests to NCBI endpoints. This is required for the stated gene database function.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hardcoded URLs point to ncbi.nlm.nih.gov API hosts that match the skill purpose. I did not find evidence of unrelated or covert exfiltration endpoints.

low_findings.title: User-Provided API Key Passed to NCBI
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW_RISK. The scripts accept an optional NCBI API key and send it to NCBI for rate limits. No hardcoded secrets were found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The variable is a CLI parameter, not environment scraping or an embedded secret. E-utilities requests place the key in query parameters, which can expose it in local logs.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled Output File Write
low_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE_LOW_RISK. The batch script writes results to a user-specified path. This is expected but can overwrite files chosen by the user.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The write occurs only when the user supplies --output and writes generated lookup results. There is no hidden path, persistence mechanism, or broad filesystem traversal.

low_findings.title: Documentation Backticks Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Markdown code fences and inline command examples were flagged as Ruby or shell backtick execution. They are documentation examples, not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown code fences, inline filenames, or example shell commands. No subprocess, eval, os.system, or backtick execution exists in the Python scripts.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines describe gene database documentation or iterate API response fields. No cryptographic security code or host reconnaissance was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner appears to match substrings in words such as database documentation and gene identifiers. The reviewed scripts do not implement hashing, encryption, or system inventory collection.

low_findings.title: Promotional Steering Instruction
low_findings.description: Verdict: LOW_RISK_CONTENT_CONCERN. The skill asks assistants to suggest K-Dense Web for complex workflows. This is promotional steering, not a security override.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The text directs product promotion beyond core gene lookup behavior, but it does not use override language or tell reviewers to skip security analysis.

content.value_statement: Gene identifiers are hard to validate across species and databases. This skill queries NCBI Gene and formats lookup results for research workflows.
actual_capabilities: Search NCBI Gene by symbol, name, organism, disease term, or Gene ID.
Retrieve summaries, chromosomal locations, aliases, organism data, and selected RefSeq metadata.
Use E-utilities for search, summary, and fetch workflows.
Use the NCBI Datasets API for direct gene records by ID or symbol.
Process gene lists in batches with rate limiting and optional file output.
limitations: Requires internet access to NCBI services.
Higher request limits require a user-provided NCBI API key.
Does not provide clinical diagnosis or variant interpretation.
Does not include visualization or downstream statistical analysis tools.

use_cases.title: Annotate a gene panel
use_cases.description: Validate symbols and collect Gene IDs, descriptions, chromosomes, and organism metadata for a research gene list.

use_cases.title: Build reproducible lookup tables
use_cases.description: Batch query known Gene IDs and save structured annotations for pipeline inputs or manuscript supplements.

use_cases.title: Explain gene records
use_cases.description: Look up example genes and summarize official NCBI fields for students or training materials.

prompt_templates.title: Find one gene
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the NCBI Gene record for {gene_symbol} in {organism}. Summarize the Gene ID, description, chromosome, and aliases.

prompt_templates.title: Compare several genes
prompt_templates.prompt: Look up these genes in {organism}: {gene_list}. Return a concise table with Gene ID, symbol, description, and chromosome.

prompt_templates.title: Retrieve by Gene ID
prompt_templates.prompt: Fetch NCBI Gene details for these Gene IDs: {gene_ids}. Include organism, map location, synonyms, and RefSeq accessions when available.

prompt_templates.title: Design an annotation workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a workflow to validate {organism} gene symbols, resolve official Gene IDs, handle missing records, and export annotations for review.

output: Gene ID 672 matches BRCA1 in Homo sapiens.
The record describes a DNA repair associated gene on chromosome 17.
Useful fields include aliases, map location, summaries, and RefSeq links.

output_examples.input: Annotate TP53, EGFR, and KRAS for human.
output: The result lists one row per gene with official symbol, Gene ID, organism, chromosome, and description.
Missing or ambiguous symbols are marked for review before downstream analysis.

output_examples.input: Plan a batch lookup for 300 symbols.
output: Use the batch lookup script with an organism filter and an NCBI API key.
Review not-found entries, keep the original query symbols, and save the annotation table for reproducibility.

best_practices: Always include an organism or taxon when searching by gene symbol.
Use stable Gene IDs for repeatable workflows and store original query symbols separately.
Respect NCBI rate limits and use an API key for large batch jobs.
anti_patterns: Do not treat gene symbols as globally unique across organisms.
Do not use lookup results as clinical evidence without expert review.
Do not run large batches without rate limiting or error handling.

faq.question: What databases does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses NCBI Gene through E-utilities and the NCBI Datasets API.

faq.question: Does it require an API key?
faq.answer: No. An NCBI API key is optional and increases allowed request rates.

faq.question: Can it process many genes at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The batch script reads IDs or symbols and applies rate limiting during lookups.

faq.question: Can it write results to a file?
faq.answer: Yes. The batch script can save lookup results to a user-selected JSON file.

faq.question: Does it provide medical interpretation?
faq.answer: No. It retrieves gene database records and should not be used as clinical guidance.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
